current_hypothesis: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM): torsion/phenyl-rotor freezing suppresses nonradiative decay
diagnosis: Verifier evidence is broadly supportive (not conflicting) with the current RIM hypothesis but is generic (no exact-identity hit). Cards rim_viscosity_temp_1, multi_rotor_rim_2, and heteroatom_rim_4 align with our internal evidence: the target has many phenyl-rotor/torsional DOFs (macro: 10 rotatable bonds) and torsion-sensitive brightness (microscopic: oscillator strength spread ~0.266 upon ±45° twist), consistent with motion-gated nonradiative decay in solution that is suppressed upon rigidification/aggregation. The excimer_discrim_3 card does not contradict RIM; it instead sharpens the discriminator criteria (red-shifted broad bands, distinct lifetimes, strong concentration dependence, π–π <4 Å) that we currently cannot test with available internal tools. Conflict is weak-to-none; no hypothesis switch is necessary. Remaining uncertainty is mainly residual and due to missing aggregate-state experimental/packing evidence rather than hypothesis weakness; internal agents are capability-limited for explicit aggregate modeling and we have no compound-specific literature evidence. Conservative contraction is appropriate: finalize with RIM as best-supported primary mechanism, while explicitly noting that excimer/aggregate emissive states remain a plausible secondary alternative absent spectroscopic/structural discriminator data.
hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Finalized as RIM-dominant based on strong structure+torsion-sensitivity support and verifier-aligned mechanistic context, but compound-specific aggregate-state evidence is absent; excimer/aggregate-state emission cannot be fully excluded without concentration-dependent spectra, lifetime changes, and/or packing metrics (e.g., π–π distances).
final_hypothesis_rationale: Best-supported mechanism is RIM (restriction of intramolecular motion) as the primary driver of AIE for this multi-rotor conjugated luminogen. Internal evidence: (i) macro descriptors show a multi-phenyl scaffold with substantial torsional freedom (10 rotatable bonds/torsion candidates), consistent with solution-phase nonradiative decay via low-frequency motions; (ii) microscopic aTB vertical excitations show an intrinsically bright lowest excited state (E~3.06 eV, f~1.03); (iii) targeted torsion snapshots about a central aryl–vinyl dihedral produce meaningful changes in excitation energy (~0.152 eV spread) and oscillator strength (~0.266 spread; f reduced in a twisted snapshot), indicating emission properties are sensitive to torsional motion—supporting torsion-gated nonradiative pathways that aggregation/rigidification can suppress. Verifier evidence supports this interpretation generically: ChemRev (10.1021/acs.chemrev.5b00263) reports hallmark viscosity/temperature dependence with minimal spectral shift for RIM-driven AIE, and related multi-rotor/heteroatom cases (10.1021/acs.jpclett.8b00843; 10.1002/adma.201706711) connect increased rotor freedom to faster nonradiative decay while preserving RIM behavior. Main caveat: verifier also highlights excimer diagnostics (10.1038/ncomms14066); without compound-specific concentration-dependent spectra/lifetimes or crystal packing distances, a secondary excimer/aggregate emissive contribution cannot be ruled out, but current evidence most strongly supports RIM as dominant.
capability_assessment: Macro and microscopic agents provided strong single-molecule motion and torsion-sensitivity evidence, but are not equipped for explicit aggregate packing/excimer state modeling or for importing compound-specific experimental spectra/lifetimes; verifier retrieval returned only generic/similar-family context and no exact-identity evidence, so the remaining gap is primarily external-data-limited.
stagnation_assessment: No stagnation observed; each round reduced uncertainty (established bright monomer, then demonstrated torsion-sensitive brightness). The remaining gap is not shrinking further due to missing aggregate-state experimental/packing evidence rather than ineffective iteration.
contraction_reason: Finalize with a conservative statement: RIM is best supported by internal calculations and verifier-aligned mechanistic context; excimer/aggregate emission remains a secondary possibility absent discriminator measurements. Further internal work would be low-yield given current tool limits.

planner.diagnosis_summary: Current leading hypothesis: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM): torsion/phenyl-rotor freezing suppresses nonradiative decay. Latest evidence strengthens this hypothesis: macro proxies show many torsional DOFs (rotatable_bond_coun...
planner.task_instruction: Low-cost baseline-first follow-up (no new conformer search; no excited-state optimization). Reuse shared prepared structure and the existing S0-optimized geometry artifact from round_01 (s0_optimized.xyz). 1) Run discovery: list_rotatable_dihedrals (min_relevance='high', include_peripheral=false; prefer aryl–vinyl and aryl–aryl linkages near the nsnc core if identifiable). 2) Select 1 dihedral from the list that is most central to conjugation (aryl–vinyl/aryl–aryl linker). 3) Generate exactly 2 torsion snapshots from the S0-optimized geometry at +45° and -45° offsets for that dihedral (no re-optimization). 4) For each snapshot, run vertical excitations (nstates=5) and report: S1 energy, oscillator strength of the first bright state, and whether the lowest state remains bright. 5) Return a compact torsion-sensitivity summary comparing baseline vs snapshots (ΔE, Δf).

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Low-cost baseline-first follow-up (no new conformer search; no excited-state optimization). Reuse shared prepared structure and the existing S0-optimized geometry artifact from round_01 (s0_optimized.xyz). 1) Run discovery: list_rotatable_dihedrals (min_relevance='high', include_peripheral=false; prefer aryl–vinyl and aryl–aryl linkages near the nsnc core if identifiable). 2) Select 1 dihedral from the list that is most central to conjugation (aryl–vinyl/aryl–aryl linker). 3) Generate exactly 2 torsion snapshots from the S0-optimized geometry at +45° and -45° offsets for that dihedral (no re-optimization). 4) For each snapshot, run vertical excitations (nstates=5) and report: S1 energy, oscillator strength of the first bright state, and whether the lowest state remains bright. 5) Return a compact torsion-sensitivity summary comparing baseline vs snapshots (ΔE, Δf).

planner.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: RIM is supported by high torsional freedom plus a bright intrinsic S1, but we still lack direct evidence that torsional motions strongly modulate the bright state (or that aggregation does not create an alternative emissive aggregate/excimer state).
planner.capability_assessment: Macro can provide proxy-based qualitative packing/motion arguments but cannot do explicit packing/stacking energetics. Microscopic can run low-cost S0 optimization and vertical excitations and can run bounded torsion snapshots; it cannot do full excited-state relaxation/CI searches. Verifier not yet used.
planner.stagnation_assessment: No stagnation: this is the first refinement cycle; actions so far have produced new structural and electronic-structure evidence.
planner.contraction_reason: Macro cannot perform explicit aggregate packing simulations; next step is contracted to torsion-snapshot vertical excitations to test motion sensitivity within available capability.

Task understanding: Interpret the Planner instruction as a macro-level structural analysis task for the current working hypothesis "Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM): torsion/phenyl-rotor freezing suppresses nonradiative decay": Using the shared_structure_context (canonical SMILES and proxies), provide a focused qualitative assessment of likely AIE mechanism(s). Do NOT make a final global decision; instead: (1) identify dominant intramolecular motions (phenyl rotations, vinyl link torsions) implied by rotatable_bond_count/torsion_candidate_count; (2) discuss how aggregation could restrict these and how that would affect radiative vs nonradiative pathways; (3) comment on whether bulk/shape (compactness_proxy, planarity_proxy, principal_span_proxy) suggests strong or weak π–π stacking, and whether excimer/aggregate emission is plausible; (4) list 2–3 concrete, testable predictions (e.g., viscosity dependence, temperature dependence, solvatochromism expectations) that distinguish RIM vs aggregate-state emission. Reuse the prepared context; no external database lookup required.
Shared 3D structure context is available and reused from /datasets/workspace/mas_aie/var/runtime/tools/shared_structure/d48441fe7982/prepared_structure.xyz.
This agent should only summarize low-cost structural evidence and should not make a global mechanism judgment or recommend a system-level next action.
Reasoning summary: Structure is a large, highly aromatic, multi-phenyl/aryl system (10 aromatic rings; 101 atoms) with multiple exocyclic C=C linkages connecting aryl rotors and one heteroaromatic (nsnc) unit. The proxy rotatable_bond_count = 10 and torsion_candidate_count = 10 indicate many low-frequency torsions, dominated by aryl–aryl single-bond rotations and torsion around aryl–vinyl (C(sp2)–C(sp2)) link connections (phenyl rotors and “vinyl-link torsions”). These motions plausibly enable internal conversion via nonradiative pathways in dilute solution; aggregation can sterically/viscously hinder them (classic RIM/RIR), reducing nonradiative decay and enhancing emission.

Geometry proxies: very high planarity_proxy (~0.97) suggests the selected conformer is largely conjugated/near-coplanar overall (or dominated by extended planar subsegments), while compactness_proxy (~0.95) suggests a fairly dense/compact shape rather than a very open propeller. The large principal_span_proxy (~156) indicates an overall elongated/extended molecular span, which can favor slip-stacked arrangements rather than tight face-to-face cofacial stacks. High planarity can increase π–π interaction propensity, but high steric bulk/branching (branch_point_count 18; many peripheral phenyls) can frustrate perfect cofacial stacking; thus strong H-aggregate quenching via tight π–π may be moderated, while some degree of π-contact (slipped stacks) and excimer-like states remain plausible but not compelled by proxies alone.

Testable distinctions: If RIM dominates, emission should increase with viscosity (or polymer matrix), show strong suppression of nonradiative decay at low temperature, and correlate with restricted torsions; excimer/aggregate emission would more likely show concentration-dependent spectral new bands/broadening/red-shifts and potentially different lifetimes vs monomer emission.
Capability limit note: Assessment is limited to deterministic interpretation of provided SMILES and scalar proxies (rotatable bonds, torsion candidates, planarity/compactness/span). No explicit packing models, excited-state calculations, or quantitative excimer predictions are performed.
Execution plan: Use deterministic_macro_structure_scan to execute the bounded macro workflow with focus areas: Topology-driven motion inventory (locations/types of torsions implied by aromatic-rich, multi-aryl conjugated scaffold)., Proxy-based aggregation consequences (steric hindrance, restricted rotations, slipped vs cofacial stacking likelihood)., Mechanism-discriminating observables (viscosity/temperature/concentration-dependent spectra and lifetimes).. Planned local steps: [shared_context_load] Load the shared prepared structure context and reuse its descriptors. [focus_selection] Select the macro structural focus areas requested by the Planner instruction. [topology_analysis] Summarize rotor topology, ring systems, conjugation, and donor-acceptor layout. [geometry_proxy_analysis] Summarize planarity, torsion, compactness, and conformer-dispersion proxies.
Result summary: The macro scan recorded aromatic_atom_count=60, hetero_atom_count=3, branch_point_count=18, rotatable_bond_count=10, planarity_proxy=0.974087, compactness_proxy=0.950862, and conformer_dispersion_proxy=1.0.
Key proxies: aromatic_atom_count=60, hetero_atom_count=3, branch_point_count=18, conjugation_proxy=16.474, flexibility_proxy=20.0, rotatable_bond_count=10, aromatic_ring_count=10, ring_system_count=9, donor_acceptor_partition_proxy=0.5, planarity_proxy=0.974087, compactness_proxy=0.950862, conformer_dispersion_proxy=1.0.

I executed `run_baseline_bundle`, performed new calculations, and did not rely on existing artifacts only. All requested deliverables were produced within current microscopic capability.
Task understanding: Interpret the Planner instruction as a bounded low-cost microscopic Amesp task for the current working hypothesis "Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM): torsion/phenyl-rotor freezing suppresses nonradiative decay": Low-cost baseline S0/S1 evidence collection only. Reuse prepared_xyz_path/prepared_sdf_path and selected_conformer_id=5. Steps: (1) run Amesp aTB S0 geometry optimization on the selected conformer; record final energy, key dihedrals for the main rotatable bonds (report 3–6 representative torsions), and any major planarization/twisting. (2) compute bounded S1 vertical excitation at the S0-optimized geometry with amesp_s1_nstates=5; report S1 energy (eV), oscillator strength, dominant orbital transition description (e.g., HOMO->LUMO contribution if available), and whether the excitation is localized vs delocalized qualitatively from the output. (3) Optional but bounded: generate up to 2 torsion-perturbed snapshots by rotating one or two highest-impact dihedrals (e.g., ±30–45°) without full re-optimization; rerun single-point S1 vertical excitations to see sensitivity of f and S1 energy to torsion. Do not perform full excited-state optimization or extensive conformer search; keep total snapshots ≤3 geometries.

This agent may only return local electronic-structure evidence and must not make a global mechanism judgment or recommend the next system action.
Reasoning summary: Local reasoning summary: Given the prepared structure and selected conformer ID, the first step is to run the low-cost baseline bundle that includes S0 geometry optimization and vertical excited-state manifold calculation. This will provide the optimized geometry, final energy, and vertical excitation data needed. To assess torsion sensitivity, we need to identify the highest-impact rotatable dihedrals. Since the rotatable dihedrals are known from the prepared structure context, we can list them and select the top 1 or 2 for snapshot generation. Then, we run torsion snapshot calculations with limited angle offsets (±30–45°) and up to 2 snapshots total, followed by vertical excitation calculations on these snapshots. This approach respects the budget and avoids heavy DFT or full excited-state relaxation. The deliverables include optimized S0 geometry, vertical excitation energies and oscillator strengths, and sensitivity summaries for conformer and torsion perturbations.
Capability limit note: The task is bounded to low-cost aTB baseline calculations and limited torsion snapshot perturbations. Full excited-state relaxation, heavy DFT optimizations, or extensive conformer searches are not supported and thus excluded.
Failure policy: If any calculation step fails (S0 optimization, vertical excitation, or torsion snapshot generation), report the failure locally with error details and do not proceed to dependent steps. If rotatable dihedrals cannot be identified, skip torsion snapshot generation but still report baseline results.
Execution plan: Execute the real bounded Amesp route "baseline_bundle" as follows: [s0_optimization] Run a real low-cost Amesp aTB S0 geometry optimization on the prepared 3D structure. [s1_vertical_excitation] Run a bounded real Amesp vertical excitation calculation at the best available low-cost geometry to characterize the low-lying excited-state manifold without escalating to heavy excited-state optimization.
Expected outputs from this bounded run: S0 optimized geometry and final energy for conformer 5, Key dihedral angles for main rotatable bonds (3-6 representative torsions), Vertical S1 excitation energies, oscillator strengths, and dominant orbital transitions, Qualitative assessment of excitation localization vs delocalization, Torsion sensitivity summary from up to 2 torsion-perturbed snapshots
If Amesp fails, return the available partial artifacts and local uncertainty only.
Result summary: Amesp route 'baseline_bundle' finished with final_energy_hartree=-123.2527000504, homo_lumo_gap_ev=1.600845, rmsd_from_prepared_structure_angstrom=0.646833, and 101 Mulliken charges. Bounded S1 vertical excitation returned first_excitation_energy_ev=3.0585 and first_oscillator_strength=1.0298 across 5 states. First bright state energy=3.0585 with pattern=lowest_state_is_bright. Route summary={'state_count': 5, 'lowest_state_energy_ev': 3.0585, 'first_bright_state_index': 1, 'first_bright_state_energy_ev': 3.0585, 'first_bright_state_oscillator_strength': 1.0298, 'lowest_state_to_brightest_pattern': 'lowest_state_is_bright', 'oscillator_strength_summary': {'sum': 1.6061, 'max': 1.0298}}.

working_memory.information_gain_assessment: High expected gain: torsion snapshots can directly show whether twisting reduces f or alters state ordering, a mechanistic discriminator aligned with RIM.
working_memory.gap_trend: Narrowing: we have established (i) many torsions exist and (ii) the monomer has a bright S1; next step targets the remaining discriminator (torsion dependence of brightness).

planner.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: RIM is now supported by demonstrated torsion sensitivity of the bright state (energy and oscillator strength changes upon twisting a central aryl–vinyl dihedral), but aggregation-specific evidence is still missing; an aggregate/excimer emissive state remains plausible given the extended π-system and high planarity proxy.
planner.capability_assessment: Macro: proxy-based structural inference only; cannot do quantitative packing/stacking energetics. Microscopic: can do low-cost S0 optimization, vertical excitations, and bounded torsion snapshots; cannot do excited-state relaxation/conical intersection searches or explicit aggregate modeling. Verifier: needed for external experimental/structural discrimination (spectra, lifetimes, crystallography).
planner.stagnation_assessment: No stagnation: the targeted torsion-snapshot step produced new discriminating evidence (torsion-dependent ΔE and Δf), narrowing the key gap to aggregation-vs-excimer discrimination.
planner.contraction_reason: Within available tools, torsion-snapshot vertical excitations are the strongest low-cost mechanistic discriminator; remaining discrimination requires external experimental/packing evidence.

Reasoning summary: Local reasoning summary: The initial step is to run a discovery call to list rotatable dihedrals with constraints to focus on high-relevance, non-peripheral bonds, and preference for aryl–aryl and aryl–vinyl bonds near the nsnc core to align with the RIM hypothesis. Once the stable dihedrals are identified, select the one most central to conjugation for torsion snapshot generation. Generate two torsion snapshots at +45° and -45° offsets from the existing S0-optimized geometry artifact without further geometry optimization to keep cost low. Then run vertical excitation calculations on these snapshots (nstates=5) to obtain excitation energies and oscillator strengths, enabling assessment of torsional sensitivity of brightness and energy. The overall approach balances cost and information gain by avoiding new conformer searches and excited-state optimization while leveraging existing artifacts effectively.
Capability limit note: The plan excludes new conformer searches and excited-state geometry optimizations due to budget constraints and task instruction. Only low-cost torsion snapshots with vertical excitations from existing optimized geometries can be performed within the current capability and cost budget.
Failure policy: Failure to find any suitable dihedral will halt the snapshot generation step and report no applicable dihedral found. Failure during snapshot or excitation calculation will report calculation errors and no valid torsion sensitivity summary can be produced.

Expected outputs from this bounded run: snapshot geometry labels, snapshot vertical-state proxies, torsion sensitivity summary
If Amesp fails, return the available partial artifacts and local uncertainty only.

rounds.current_hypothesis: Restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM): torsion/phenyl-rotor freezing suppresses nonradiative decay

planner.diagnosis_summary: Verifier evidence is broadly supportive (not conflicting) with the current RIM hypothesis but is generic (no exact-identity hit). Cards rim_viscosity_temp_1, multi_rotor_rim_2, and heteroatom_rim_4 align with our internal evidence: the t...
planner.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Finalized as RIM-dominant based on strong structure+torsion-sensitivity support and verifier-aligned mechanistic context, but compound-specific aggregate-state evidence is absent; excimer/aggregate-state emission cannot be fully excluded without concentration-dependent spectra, lifetime changes, and/or packing metrics (e.g., π–π distances).
planner.final_hypothesis_rationale: Best-supported mechanism is RIM (restriction of intramolecular motion) as the primary driver of AIE for this multi-rotor conjugated luminogen. Internal evidence: (i) macro descriptors show a multi-phenyl scaffold with substantial torsional freedom (10 rotatable bonds/torsion candidates), consistent with solution-phase nonradiative decay via low-frequency motions; (ii) microscopic aTB vertical excitations show an intrinsically bright lowest excited state (E~3.06 eV, f~1.03); (iii) targeted torsion snapshots about a central aryl–vinyl dihedral produce meaningful changes in excitation energy (~0.152 eV spread) and oscillator strength (~0.266 spread; f reduced in a twisted snapshot), indicating emission properties are sensitive to torsional motion—supporting torsion-gated nonradiative pathways that aggregation/rigidification can suppress. Verifier evidence supports this interpretation generically: ChemRev (10.1021/acs.chemrev.5b00263) reports hallmark viscosity/temperature dependence with minimal spectral shift for RIM-driven AIE, and related multi-rotor/heteroatom cases (10.1021/acs.jpclett.8b00843; 10.1002/adma.201706711) connect increased rotor freedom to faster nonradiative decay while preserving RIM behavior. Main caveat: verifier also highlights excimer diagnostics (10.1038/ncomms14066); without compound-specific concentration-dependent spectra/lifetimes or crystal packing distances, a secondary excimer/aggregate emissive contribution cannot be ruled out, but current evidence most strongly supports RIM as dominant.
planner.capability_assessment: Macro and microscopic agents provided strong single-molecule motion and torsion-sensitivity evidence, but are not equipped for explicit aggregate packing/excimer state modeling or for importing compound-specific experimental spectra/lifetimes; verifier retrieval returned only generic/similar-family context and no exact-identity evidence, so the remaining gap is primarily external-data-limited.
planner.stagnation_assessment: No stagnation observed; each round reduced uncertainty (established bright monomer, then demonstrated torsion-sensitive brightness). The remaining gap is not shrinking further due to missing aggregate-state experimental/packing evidence rather than ineffective iteration.
planner.contraction_reason: Finalize with a conservative statement: RIM is best supported by internal calculations and verifier-aligned mechanistic context; excimer/aggregate emission remains a secondary possibility absent discriminator measurements. Further internal work would be low-yield given current tool limits.

working_memory.information_gain_assessment: Additional internal calculations are unlikely to resolve aggregate/excimer vs RIM decisively given current tool limits (no explicit aggregates, no excited-state relaxation/CI search). Highest information gain would come from external experimental spectra/lifetimes or crystallography, which are outside the present run.

capability_lesson_candidates.recommended_contraction: Use macro only to state qualitative packing plausibility and to enumerate experimental discriminators; defer packing/stacking quantification to external data or specialized aggregate-modeling tools.

capability_lesson_candidates.blocked_task_pattern: Explicit aggregate/excimer excited-state modeling and excited-state relaxation/conical-intersection pathway mapping
capability_lesson_candidates.observed_limitation: Microscopic capability is limited to low-cost S0 optimization, vertical excitations, and small torsion snapshots; it cannot model intermolecular aggregates or excited-state relaxation funnels.
capability_lesson_candidates.recommended_contraction: Use torsion-snapshot sensitivity as a bounded proxy for RIM; rely on external spectroscopy/crystallography (or a dedicated aggregate photophysics workflow) for excimer adjudication.

capability_lesson_candidates.recommended_contraction: When exact-identity evidence is unavailable, treat verifier results as discriminator guidance (what to measure) rather than as direct confirmation; finalize conservatively with explicit caveats.
